content:
Nice puzzle!

One small thing: "The character used for an empty square may differ between the two board states" could mislead solvers into thinking there's one empty character per board. The noisy test cases actually use multiple different non-piece characters as empty squares within the same board. Maybe rephrase to: "Empty squares may be represented by different characters, even within the same board."

✅ Approved the contribution.
Comment: Tested, played, looks good

✅ Approved the contribution.
Comment: Checkmate! As a huge chess fan, I really enjoyed this one. 
The test cases are bulletproof. 100% approved, great job! :)

Title
* Why specify the "knight"?

Goal
* Please change "lines of" to "rows and".

* Please change "free" to "empty".

* Please replace

Each piece is identified with a letter (upper case for one player, lower case for the other). You just have to write down the move with its coordinates.

with:

Each piece may appear on the board in uppercase (white) or lowercase (black).

Any character not in the set of valid piece letters (in either uppercase or lowercase) represents an empty cell. The character used for an empty cell may differ between the two board states.

Your task is to output the move with its coordinates.

Input
* Please rewrite this section as follows:

<<Line 1>>: A string [[pieces]] containing the uppercase English letters that represent the white pieces. (The corresponding lowercase letters represent the black pieces.)
<<Next 8 lines>>: The board before the move.
<<Next 8 lines>>: The board after the move.

Each line of the board is a string of 8 printable ASCII characters. Each character is either a piece or an empty cell.

Output
* Please add "<<Line 1>>:" before "Coordinates".

* Please change "was move" to "was moved".

* Please format "a", "h", "1", "8", "a8", "-" and "x" as {{constants}}.

* Please add ", from left to right" after "on the board".

* Please add ", from bottom to top" after "vertical position".

* Please remove the examples.

Constraints
* Please replace "free" with "empty".

* Please remove the entire "caution" sentence as this is not a constraint (and is no longer necessary after modifying the Goal section as described above).

Difficulty
* Consider changing the difficulty from Medium to Easy.

Cases
* Please remove the trailing newlines from the input of all the cases.

* Consider changing Crazy case (Test 4) such that the output is a simple move, similar to Validator 4. This would also ensure more balanced test coverage (50% simple moves and 50% captures).

* I'll review the cases again once the above changes have been applied.

Thanks.

Many thanks for this comprehensive review and all your suggestions.  This really improves the readability of the puzzle. I have adopted them all. 
(one little exception:  the "English" letters are "Latin" letters according to ISO  (see also https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_basic_Latin_alphabet ).
I also changed "cell" to "square",  for more consistency with chess terminology and terms used in other chess puzzles. 

Regarding the title,  I wanted something specific to which the readers could relate.  Since the knights have the most complex moves, they often strike with surprise for the less advanced players. The question "but where was it before" is not uncommon in this context.
Many thanks for your positive and energizing  feedback !
Thank you for your nice feedback and approval ! Excellent suggestion:  I updated accordingly.

The new wording improves clarity to a certain extent, but it shifts the original meaning. The idea that empty-square characters may "differ between the two board states" becomes implied rather than explicit. Consider using this wording:
    
Empty squares may be represented by different characters, even within the same board, and the representation may also differ between the two board states.

Thank you for the clarification.

I understand your point regarding ISO terminology. However, the ISO basic Latin alphabet is not strictly the same as the Latin alphabet (the latter does not include J, U and W, see https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Latin_alphabet ). In other words, the term "Latin" can be interpreted in different ways. For clarity, I still believe that referring to "English letters" instead of "Latin letters" is more immediately understandable for most readers.

As for the title, I understand your reasoning about the knight. My only concern is that the word "knight" does not appear anywhere in the problem statement itself, so as a reader I found the reference confusing rather than relatable. That said, I recognise that the title is ultimately your choice, and I won't push for a change.

Thank you as well for updating the statement. Below are my suggestions to further refine the puzzle:

Input
* Please change "'A'-'Z'" to "{{A}}-{{Z}}".

Output
* In "initial and final square", please change "square" to "squares".

* Please remove the quotation marks around all the text formatted as {{constants}}.

Cases
* Test 4 and Validator 4 are the same. They should be *similar*, but slightly different. Please revise Test 4 such that all of the following conditions hold:
  • It remains a "crazy" case.
  • It remains a no-capture case.
  • The output contains two letters not used in other visible tests (a, b, f, g, h).
  • The output contains two digits not used in any other visible tests (1, 6, 7, 8).

* Consider adding additional pairs of cases to cover output coordinate letters and digits not currently used in the existing tests and validators.

Thanks again.

Thanks again for your feedback! 
I updated it accordingly. I added a case and transformed slightly the crazy case to cover more cases.   Now only digit 1 remains unused.  
I also clarified that a capture is necessarily a piece of the opponent (to prevent an unsolvable edge case where a piece would capture the same piece ("to a square occupied by another piece of the opposite player"). 
And I aded a constraint regarding the minimum size of [[pieces]].
Regarding the English vs Latin alphabet, I propose to keep it simple and just refer to "letters (A-Z)" which should be self explaining.  Alternatively, "ASCII letters (A-Z)" could do.  Indeed, zs a non native speaker, the first time I read "English alphabet", I had to double-check if it was the same international alphabet. This other article on Letters (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_(alphabet) ) refers by the way to the Latin alphabet instead of the English one ;-)

I noted your point regarding English vs Latin; I'm fine with your treatment. I also noted the other changes and have no further comments on those.

Please add a new pair of cases as follows:

Test
* The output should include a, b, 1 and 8 (not covered in any visible tests).
* Black moved without capture.
* The characters used for empty squares are always different between the two board states.

Validator
* The output should include d, h and 1 (not covered in any validators).
* Black moved without capture (not covered in any validators).
* The characters used for empty squares are always different between the two board states.

Thanks.

Great !  That's an excellent idea. I've now added a case "Lots of   noise" that meet this requirement.  As there cannot be a valid move involving a,b,1 and 8  (no diagonal move,  no rectangular knight move),  I changed one of the existing cases to land on a b square to ensure the expected coverage. 
Also, I added a second line in the output and updated the goals and the cases accordingly.
Yay, that works, everything nicely covered now (including the "Knight" in the title)! 👍
